sound_type: squeaky peeping -- soft, high-pitched, almost mouse-like. 2,000-6,000 Hz. Close-range courtship and interaction calls. Does not call in flight. E=3. The quietest entry in the Anatidae streak.
ecosystem: Lake -- winter visitor to PNW. Puget Sound bays, freshwater lakes, river mouths, harbors. Breeds in boreal Canada/Alaska near Northern Flicker cavities.
season: Winter visitor (October-April). Common on Puget Sound, Lake Washington, Lake Union, and protected coastal waters.
family: Anatidae (fifth consecutive entry: Harlequin 118, Common Merganser 119, Hooded Merganser 120, Wood Duck 121, Bufflehead 122)

coordinates.description: Reference for winter lake habitat in the Puget Sound region. Buffleheads common on Lake Washington, Lake Union, Green Lake, and Puget Sound bays in winter.

taxonomy_note.description: Fifth consecutive Anatidae entry. North America's smallest diving duck (270-450g). Male's black-and-white plumage with large white head patch on puffy 'buffalo head.' Squeaky peeping. Only duck small enough for Northern Flicker cavities. Winter visitor to PNW. Genus Bucephala (goldeneye group). E=3.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: 5 entries (Harlequin Duck 118, Common Merganser 119, Hooded Merganser 120, Wood Duck 121, Bufflehead 122). Streak: 5 consecutive.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Bucephala from Greek bous (ox) + kephale (head) -- 'ox-headed,' referencing the large, round head. albeola from Latin albus (white) in diminutive -- 'little white one.' Bufflehead from 'buffalo head' -- the disproportionately large, puffy head.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Bucephala -- three species: Bufflehead (B. albeola), Common Goldeneye (B. clangula), Barrow's Goldeneye (B. islandica). Bufflehead is the smallest by significant margin. Size-mediated niche partitioning within the genus.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes continues (fifth consecutive). The longest waterfowl run. Energy descent across the streak: E=5, 4, 4, 4, 3.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae fifth consecutive entry. Streak: 118-122. Track continuation at 123. If Anatidae continues, streak reaches 6.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 2,000-6,000 Hz. High-pitched squeaky peep. Almost mouse-like.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 3,000-5,000 Hz. The squeaky peep concentrates energy in the high-frequency range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Male: soft squeaky peep, 2,000-6,000 Hz. Female: hoarse low crrrk, softer and less frequent.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual peep: 0.1-0.3 seconds. Brief, high-pitched. Given in courtship sequences.
song_metrics.note_structure: SQUEAKY PEEPING: Soft, high-pitched, almost mouse-like. Thin and delicate. Not a quack, honk, or whistle -- a squeak. Barely audible at distance.
song_metrics.note_types: Male squeaky peep (diagnostic but soft), female hoarse crrrk, alarm calls (infrequent). Overall vocalizations are rare and quiet.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Individual squeaks in close-range interactions. No complex phrasing. No flight calls. No distance-contact calls. Intimate-range only.
song_metrics.timbre: Squeaky. Thin. High-pitched. Mouse-like. Delicate. Unlike any other common duck vocalization in its softness and pitch.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3 -- low. One of the quietest ducks in North America. Calls can be missed even at close range. Visual identification far more reliable than acoustic. The quietest entry in the Anatidae streak.
song_metrics.tempo: Infrequent. Vocalizations are given rarely compared to other ducks. Most commonly heard during courtship display.
song_metrics.context: COURTSHIP AND CLOSE-RANGE INTERACTION. Not in flight. Not for flock contact. Intimate, close-range vocalizations only.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal documented variation. Calls are species-typical.
song_metrics.key_property: SQUEAKY PEEPING -- soft, high-pitched, mouse-like. E=3. Fifth consecutive Anatidae. Smallest diving duck in NA. Only duck for flicker cavities. Buffalo head. Winter visitor. Lake ecosystem.

plumage.overall: Male: iridescent black head (purple-green in light) with large white patch from behind eye across back of head. White flanks, breast, belly. Black back. Stark black-and-white contrast. Female: dark brown-gray with smaller white oval cheek patch behind and below eye. Both: compact body, disproportionately large puffy head.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 122: (1) MALE: Large white patch on back of puffy dark head (unmistakable). (2) MALE: Clean black-and-white body. (3) FEMALE: White oval cheek patch on dark head. (4) TINY SIZE -- smallest diving duck. (5) Puffy 'buffalo head' shape.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Males in eclipse plumage resemble females but retain larger white head patch. Winter plumage (when seen in PNW) is full breeding plumage.
plumage.flight_pattern: Rapid, direct flight with very fast wingbeats. White wing patches flash. Compact, buzzing silhouette. Does not call in flight.

physical_description.overall: Smallest diving duck in North America. Body length 32-40 cm. Wingspan 54-56 cm. Weight 270-450 g. Roughly one-fifth the mass of Common Merganser. Compact, round body. Disproportionately large, puffy head.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 122: tiny diving duck with puffy dark head bearing large white patch (male) or small white cheek patch (female). If it's the smallest duck on the lake with a buffalo-shaped head -- Bufflehead.
physical_description.field_id: Tiny duck on lakes and bays. Male: dark head with large white patch, black-and-white body. Female: dark head with small white cheek patch. Both: puffy oversized head, compact body. Dives frequently. Squeaky peeping at close range.

nodes.title: The Buffalo Head -- Puffy Black-and-White Plumage
nodes.description: Disproportionately large, round head -- the 'buffalo head' that gives the species its name. Male: iridescent black with large white patch. Stark black-and-white body. Tuxedo elegance. Minimal design.

nodes.title: Squeaky Peeping -- The Quietest Duck
nodes.description: Soft, high-pitched, mouse-like peeping. 2,000-6,000 Hz. Close-range courtship only. Does not call in flight. E=3. The quietest entry in the Anatidae streak. A duck you see before you hear.

nodes.title: Anatidae Streak -- Five Consecutive (118-122)
nodes.description: Harlequin (118, E=5), Common Merganser (119, E=4), Hooded Merganser (120, E=4), Wood Duck (121, E=4), Bufflehead (122, E=3). Five genera. Energy descent from E=5 to E=3. More than half the Picidae nonet. Sound fades as water calms.

nodes.title: The Flicker Connection -- Only Duck Small Enough
nodes.description: The Bufflehead is the ONLY duck in North America small enough to nest in Northern Flicker cavities (~6-7 cm entrance). Tight, specific dependency. Flicker at degree 99; Bufflehead at degree 122. 23-degree ecological chain. The Picidae-Anatidae pipeline at its most intimate.

nodes.title: The Pocket Diver -- Smallest Diving Duck
nodes.description: 270-450g. One-fifth the mass of Common Merganser. Dives 12-25 seconds in 1-4 meters. Small prey specialist. Forward lunge, almost no splash, reappears like a cork. Shallow-water niche that larger divers overlook.

nodes.title: Goldeneye Lineage -- Genus Bucephala
nodes.description: Three species: Bufflehead, Common Goldeneye, Barrow's Goldeneye. Size-mediated niche partitioning. Bufflehead is the smallest by significant margin. Size determines cavity size, dive depth, prey size, and habitat preference.

nodes.title: Winter Visitor to PNW Lakes
nodes.description: Breeds in boreal Canada/Alaska near flicker cavities. Winters on Puget Sound bays, freshwater lakes, river mouths. October-April. One of the most commonly seen wintering ducks in the Puget Sound region.

cross_references.relationship: CAVITY CONTRAST. Both cavity nesters. Wood Duck uses Pileated Woodpecker holes (large). Bufflehead uses Flicker holes (small). Opposite ends of the size spectrum. Streak at 5.
ANATIDAE STREAK. Five consecutive: Harlequin (118), Common Merganser (119), Hooded Merganser (120), Wood Duck (121), Bufflehead (122). Five genera. Energy descent E=5 to E=3.
STREAK ORIGIN AND ENERGY DESCENT. Streak began at E=5 (turbulent rapids). At 122, descended to E=3 (quiet lake peeping). Five degrees, progressive quieting.
THE FLICKER CONNECTION. Only duck small enough for Flicker cavities. Tight, specific dependency. 23-degree separation (99 to 122). The cavity pipeline at its most intimate.
CAVITY INFRASTRUCTURE. Nine woodpeckers (107-115) built cavity infrastructure. Five Anatidae (118-122) nest in it. Fire → woodpeckers → waterfowl. Ecological succession across 16 degrees.
